title: Note rows after select_one YN with conditional language auto-gate on the YN trigger
description: Note rows that immediately follow a select_one YN field, where the note text contains 'If yes' or similar conditional language, gate themselves with a relevant clause referencing the preceding YN field. The note displays only when the YN answer is 'Y'. AUTO-DETECTION: the skill detects the pattern when (a) a note row immediately follows a select_one YN (or YNU) row, AND (b) the note label contains the literal text 'If yes' (case-insensitive), 'if applicable', 'if so', or similar conditional preface. The preceding YN row's name is captured as the trigger. AUTO-APPLIED BEHAVIOR: set relevant=`${preceding_yn_name}='Y'`. This reduces visual clutter — the operator only sees the reminder when it applies. Matches customer authoring pattern observed across forms. Override: customer library leaves a paired note ungated; carry forward with FLAGGED status and emit a review_flags.note_gating_review entry. UNIVERSAL SCOPE per §0.
rationale: Reminder notes that always display — regardless of whether the gating Y/N is yes — create visual clutter and weaken the signal value of the reminder. Operators tune them out. Gating the note on the Y/N answer means the reminder only appears when it's actually relevant, preserving its attention-grabbing function. The 'If yes' / 'if applicable' / 'if so' prose is the linguistic signal that the note's content is conditional on the preceding question's answer; the gate makes that conditional behavior structural rather than just textual.

flag.message: Note ${field.name} on ${form.form_id} contains conditional language ('If yes' / 'if applicable' / 'if so') immediately following a select_one YN row. Auto-applied gating: set field.relevant="${<preceding_yn>}='Y'" where <preceding_yn> is the name of the immediately preceding YN row. Verify the gating target is correct.

effect.soft: Identify the immediately-preceding select_one YN or YNU row in the same form. Set this note's relevant clause to `${<that_yn_name>}='Y'`. Note that sibling-name resolution in effect expressions isn't yet a DSL primitive (per the B.1c gaps list), so the relevant string-interpolation is delegated to soft. Once string-interpolation lands as an effect feature, the directive promotes to a fully-structured 'set field.relevant' with the interpolated YN name.

history.note: Imported from conventions.md §22 during Phase B.1d. Concrete application of OC-7 sub-rule 7O.a (YES-BRANCH idiom in clinical_patterns.universal_relevance_patterns). 7O.a documents the general 'gate on YN=Y' idiom as part of an advisory library; §22 is the specific auto-detection-and-apply rule for the note-after-YN pattern. Both coexist: 7O.a as the idiom catalog, §22 as the mechanical convention. Structured detection (has_sibling with preceding_in_form scope from B.1c-1, plus matches on the conditional-language patterns). The require directive raises the flag if the note doesn't have a relevant clause; the soft directive carries the construction guidance for the relevant expression. The sibling-name-interpolation-in-effect gap from the B.1c plan doc is the limiter preventing fully-structured set.

Corrected kind from 'structured' to 'hybrid' during B.1d validation pass. The require directive on field.relevant was incompatible with the soft directive under the schema's structured-vs-soft mutual-exclusion rule. The detection IS structured (has_sibling + label-matches), but the relevant-string construction requires sibling-name interpolation which is a known DSL gap, so the effect stays in soft until that lands. require dropped; soft carries the gating guidance. Once string-interpolation lands as an effect feature, this can promote back to structured with set field.relevant.
